sentences.text: When the Nazis came into Lodz-- they, they did it all over.
But when they came to Lodz, they ordered all the Jews to put on the Star of David, with the word Jude.
And we had to stitch it on every piece of clothing.
We had to pay for it.
And they later put us into the ghetto.
But they, in the same time, they proclaimed that anyone, that anybody that had German ancestors is a Volksdeutsche, a German outside of Germany.
And then Mrs.
Gruber remembered that she's a Volksdeutsche.
We never knew that.
And that didn't mean anything.
What, what's a Volksdeutsche?
There was a new vocabulary.
And she took whatever she could, because we lived in the area that became the ghetto.
So she took whatever she could of value and not very politely, either.
She came into our house.
I had some aunts.
I had my mother's sister.
Her husband was a furrier.
And he lived in a very nice section of the city of Lodz, the nicest section, Piotrkowska.
So some of his fur coats were in our house because he escaped to [NON-ENGLISH], to, to the Polish border.
And Mrs.
Gruber knew that.
And she came in and opened the closet and took all the fur coats.
You don't need it.
She took whatever she could of value.
And they moved out to the nicest section of the city of Lodz.
And she would became real nasty.
At one point, we had a beautiful oak tree.
And that was the joy of the neighborhood.
We had it in our yard, the, the oak tree.
And there was so many stories-- people sitting under the tree at night when it was hot and talking and-- politics.
Most Jews talk politics.
[LAUGHS] It was wonderful.
But then when she was going out of the ghetto, she go-- right before it became the ghetto, she-- one day, she says, I'm going to come and knock down the tree.
I don't want the Jews to have the pleasure of the tree.
Those were her people she lived with.
And my grandmother was her best friend.
My mother was her daughter's best friend.
I was a child with this family.
Now here she was going to knock down the tree because the Jews shouldn't enjoy it.
And I remember my mother getting very angry.
She said, you, you ruined our lives.
I don't remember the wording exactly.
I have it written down in the book.
You could take your tree.
It so happened that the ghetto was closed up, and she could never knock down the tree.
And despite her, the tree stayed there until we knocked it down for firewood because we needed, we needed wood.
And [?
Harry-- ?] [?
Harry ?] was brainwashed.
He was 13 years old, grew up with us, spoke Yiddish.
He loved Jewish history, loved-- he used to say that he's going to marry me.
And I said, no,  you can't marry me.
You're not a Jew.
I knew he wasn't a Jew.
Big deal.
So I convert and become a Jew.
There was nothing to it.
And when they moved out of the area that became the ghetto, he came back only three months later, and he was dressed in the brown uniform of the Hitler Youth.
He looked like a poster boy for, for Hitler's advertisement because he had blue eyes.
He had blond hair.
He had a nightstick in his hand.
And he repeated the same ugly words what we heard on the radio.
Jews are parasites.
We have the, the, the evil of the world.
We have to destroy them.
And I just looked at him.
And he spoke all that in Yiddish.
And I looked at him and said, [?
Harry, ?] how can you talk like that?
You're my brother.
At one point, my mother saved his life because they accused him of being a spy because he was, he wasn't Jew-- he was German.
They found out then, found out that he was German.
He was ashamed just for a moment.
And then he said, now I am a German.
And I have to do what's good for Germany.
I found out after liberation that he died on the German front.
But he was brainwashed.
And he was taught to hate, and it only took three months to teach him to [INAUDIBLE].
And it's scary every time when I think of it, that it didn't take that much to teach him to hate the people that he loved.
So those were our examples of Mrs.
Gruber's.
In fact, there was another time.
It was December, and the Jews were supposed to be deported from the area before they locked us into the ghetto.
And Mrs.
Gruber came.
We had a farmer.
His name was Mr.
Brown.
He would deliver potatoes and-- that we would storage for the winter.
The winters are very cold in Poland.
And he came.
My mother had just bought a new tile oven.
It was freestanding, and it was new.
It was a luxury item.
And he saw that because he used to deliver the potatoes.
He came and he wants that oven.
They could come and just take whatever they want.
And my mother started screaming, Mrs.
Gruber, you're my friend.
Don't let him take the oven.
She said, I'll give it to him after the winter.
But it's December.
Mm-mm, she didn't do anything.
And the funny part is he took that oven, put it around with string, took it to the wagon, and it fell apart.
So [LAUGHS] Montezuma's revenge-- he didn't, he didn't have the oven.
We didn't have the oven.
But she just stood by, didn't do anything.
But-- so whatever, I don't know if she suddenly-- because she got the power or the, the, the, the hate, did the anti-Semitism was there, and it just blossomed when she had a chance?
But before that there was no anti-Semitism from her.
She was [INAUDIBLE].
She was like a nasty grandmother.
That's, that's what we thought.
Because she kept always bossing us around, very strict.
But she was there.
She was-- for better or for worse, she was helpful and sitting with my mother and talking things and friends advice.
It's scary to see how people could change.
[SIGHS] She was very upset and very disappointed because she kind of-- she was close to that woman.
She knew her all her life-- very upsetting.
And she felt very betrayed.
And she was.
She was betrayed.
I felt the same thing, betrayed from [?
Harry, ?] when he could come and tell us the Jews are parasites.
Jews are responsible for the evil.
But you were raised like a Jewish child here.
He was more Jew than whatever.
They never went to church.
The only time they celebrate it was Christmas.
And he had the Christmas tree.
And of course, I didn't have a Christmas tree.
And I had fun helping him decorate it because Mrs.
Gruber, being that she had more money, she would buy the ornaments and chocolate.
They didn't have plastic then anyway.
But she made it from chocolate with those little loops.
And [?
Harry ?] and I had a picnic.
We were hanging the chocolate.
We were supposed to hang the ornaments.
We would kind of pull, accidentally on purpose, that little loop, and we had-- we could eat chocolate for long time.
And it was a fun holiday for us.
I remember Santa Claus coming to him.
And we knew who was Santa Claus.
So it just was kind of fun.
The Jews in Poland did not look forward to Christmas or to Easter.
That was the time attack the Jew, because you're responsible for, for Jesus is dead and all the same hate stuff that still goes on in Poland.
But we didn't feel it, not from them.
We felt it around us.
We knew of it.
But not from them.
From them, Christmas was a fun holiday, and they always observed our holidays with us.
No, culturally, culturally.
We-- by religiously, you mean if we went to temple.
No, we didn't go to temple.
We-- all the holidays were observed historically, religiously.
We would sing the songs and eat the foods and talk about the holidays.
We would light candles.
We sometimes did the-- and [?
Tasha ?] would light the candles because Mommy wasn't home in time to do it.
And Mrs.
Gruber would come.
The candles are not lit.
So [?
Aunt ?] [?
Tasha ?] lit the candles.
[LAUGHS] But we had a rich cultural life, without joining a temple or going to, to religious services.
No.
No.
Ham, yeah, not pork, not pork.
Ham we did, you know, not too often.
But you know, we, we would eat it.
No.
No, I don't know of anybody in Europe eating shellfish.
I don't think it's a dish that is popular there, no.
No, I don't know if anybody did.
We didn't eat shellfish, not because it was kosher or not.
We just-- it wasn't our, our kind of food.
Yeah.
Yeah, the people that we were surrounded with were all from the same group, with the same ideas, and the same thing as the religious people would have their own cliques.
And maybe they didn't approve of our lifestyle.
And we didn't approve their lifestyle because to, to us it felt like, you know, it's a little going back a hundred years.
They're not modern enough.
But each group kind of had their own little world that they, that they lived in.
Same thing as the different organizations-- everybody had their own ideas, and each one thought that their idea is the best one, and they're each right.
And then Hitler came and said, you don't have any ideas.
He didn't care if you were religious or not religious.
[SIGHS] He took them all to the gas chambers.
A quarter of a million.
That's a large group.
I think that it was close to a million.
But I don't know exactly as far as numbers go.
But I think about a quarter-- about a million [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
They weren't there by design.
I, I think it was more by choice, where people who kind of clicked together.
And Jewish areas were Jewish areas because, you know, there was my family there.
The families used to live nearby.
So, there, you know, was large families and, and all their extended relatives, whatever.
And not by design, but the areas were kind of segregated.
Not too often, only if we went on school trips.
Like if we went on a school trip, we went to Warsaw, you know, to other big cities.
I never saw a shtetl.
I know lots of times they say shtetl.
And some people that don't know Europe think that everything was shtetls.
I never saw a shtetl.
I lived in a big city.
And I never went to a shtetl.
We traveled to mostly other big cities, like exchange programs from school or organizations or whatever.
Well, we heard bombs in 1939.
We heard-- they announced that we have to put on gas masks, that they're bombing.
And that was a shock because we expected Poland to be so strong.
That's what they kept on telling us.
Poland is strong, and nobody could destroy Poland.
We're not afraid of the big, bad wolf.
And they, they told us, to, you know, to darken the houses and prepare gas masks.
So we knew that things are kind of getting close.
We didn't know yet that it is a war.
And it just happened, like, overnight.
The, the Germans marched in, and everything changed-- whole life changed.
Could you describe what changed?
[SIGHS] Jews were thrown out of the houses if they lived in areas that was the nicest part of the city.
And Jews were stopped in the street, especially people with beards.
There were-- a religious Jew would wear a beard, and that's how we recognized a religious Jew, where the secular Jew was clean-shaven.
And they started pulling by the beards or lighting the beard with matches, grabbing people in the streets and just beating them or making them scrub the streets with their hands.
Yeah.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the beating-- everything happened suddenly.
There was one time I was caught.
This was before we were put into the ghetto.
I was going to mail a letter to a-- my father's brothers and sisters all went to Argentina years ago.
My mother didn't want to leave home so she remained.
My father remained, and we all remained in Poland.
And as we were going-- I was going with my cousin Sabcia.
And it was the first time that we were actually caught.
We were surrounded and marched through the whole day, back and forth, back and forth.
We called it a [NON-ENGLISH], that as it became known or the [NON-ENGLISH].
And we were being beaten and marched and not known where we were going.
Yeah, with a whole bunch of-- a whole group of people.
In fact, I got hit, and she just disappeared because I was knocked off the sidewalk.
And I didn't see her till later because after the marching had ended, they took away the men.
And they took them away someplace to send them away.
Later we found out they were sent to other places, to work camps, supposedly.
The women and children were left.
And we came back to the area that was designated as the ghetto.
And that's why I found Sabcia, because she managed to sneak away, and she made her way back to the area where we lived.
And I was-- [SIGHS] I came back to the ghetto, and that's when-- but it wasn't a ghetto yet-- the ghetto-designated area.
And that's where I find her.
So that, that-- firsthand, I, I saw the beating [INAUDIBLE].
Who was doing all the beating?
Germans.
German soldiers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German soldiers.
Scared, angry, bewildered.
Why is-- why-- what are they doing, and why are they doing it?
Because there were children [INAUDIBLE], old people.
There-- a mixture of people just because they were a Jew.
And it had nothing to do that, you know, I need that people for work.
I need this for this.
Just because you were a Jew, you were caught in that [NON-ENGLISH].
She said during the confusion, when they were pushing and shoving, I don't know, she kind of snuck away.
Yeah, I was hit.
And I kind of passed out, and so I didn't know that she snuck away, that she wasn't there.
Before they put us in the ghetto?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Not that I was caught in any of those incident, because since then, my mother wouldn't let me go too far from home.
That was the only time that she was-- I was going to go to the main post office because you had to mail a letter to Argentina, so you had to go to the main post office.
So I wouldn't go too far from home.
I was-- but other people were being caught, and they would disappear.
That's when my mother decided that-- to send away her teenagers.
My sister Mala had just gotten married-- and Chana and Yankele.
Because she, she was afraid because if teenagers, they might take them for hard labor.
And she was a widow with the four younger children.
I was only 13.
I was the oldest of the four youngest.
So she, she wasn't worried about us.
Who's going to bother a widow with small children?
And she used to say, well, the Germans were here the First World War.
They didn't do anything.
The world won't be silent.
She had all those wonderful sayings-- full of beliefs.
But she insisted that they should smuggle their way out of Poland, and they smuggled their way out of Poland into Russia.
At one point, they would send-- [INAUDIBLE] twice.
They had sent smugglers.
But my mother wouldn't want to move.
[DRINKS]
She said we have to be here.
When they come back, they need a home.
Where will they go?
So, so she stayed.
Then we were put into the ghetto.
We couldn't go anyplace.
How were you physically put into the ghetto?
Well, we lived in that area that became the ghetto.
So we just stayed where we were at, at that moment.
But people that lived outside the area that became ghetto, they were ordered at gunpoint.
They were just allowed to take whatever they could carry, sometimes, not always.
And they were marched into the area that became the ghetto.
And if they didn't follow orders, they would get beaten.
Yes.
My aunt had moved in with five children.
And [?
Aunt ?] [?
Tasha ?] had to leave, so she wasn't there anymore.
And there was another couple, friends of my mother, that had to move in.
So we were fortunate that we knew the people that moved in.
Some families just didn't know.
They were just assigned.
The ghetto quickly became a government.
And they were assigned.
Those people will move in with you and-- because there's no choice.
So we were fortunate because we knew the people that moved in with us.
What happened to your mother's business?
As soon as the-- first of all, when the ghetto was closed, all the sewing machines had to been given up to Germans.
So she, she had to give away all her sewing machines.
There was no private industry.
There was nothing-- no work, no jobs, no schools.
So she was just left.
Then there is no business.
She gave away the machines.
And after a while, they gave her a job when they started opening factories in the ghetto.
They give her a job as a supervisor of a factory because of her experience.

But the machines-- electrical-- well, we didn't have that many electrical-- no electrical appliance but sewing machines, radios, anything that was, at that time, electrically operated, jewelry, clothing-- everything had to be given up.
So they were left with nothing.
And no one had any business, any factories, any jobs, nothing.
Did your mother get paid for this work?
First of all, it was supposed to be security, working.
So there was a question of paying.
In the beginning, they would give them an extra ration of food, which was, at that time, was payment.
And they gave some money.
It became ghetto money.
We called it a Rumki for Rumkowski-- short for Rumkowski.
And they would get that money.
So when they had to get the ration that is assigned, they paid with that particular money.
And this was only the fortunate ones in the beginning that would get those jobs.
And then they started the deportations.
And then everybody had to work.
To stay in the ghetto, you had to, had to work, so I was taught to use a sewing machine.
In fact, I went in yesterday to a dressmaker across the street.
She needed to fix something for me for my granddaughter's wedding.
[LAUGHS] She had to just fix my gown for me.
So I was, oh, my god, this is the sewing machine I remember, one of those huge sewing machines.
The tailor shops only would have it.
And I was taught to use that machine, and I was too little to reach that machine.
It was so huge.
I don't know.
It didn't look that big yesterday.
[LAUGHS] They looked bigger then.
And I was making parts to the uniforms there, sewing whichever sleeves.
I don't know, whichever parts.
It was made out of a heavy blanket fabric.
The, the fabric by itself was very, very heavy.
I already had attacks of gallstones.
I had gallstones since I was 16 years old, actually before that.
And my mother would take me to the doctor.
And they said, I never saw a child having gallstones.
But in the ghetto, everything is possible.
But I had no medication.
And I would get attacks, and I was weak.
So my-- there's somebody in that factory, Mr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was-- belonged to the union together with my mother and my father, to the labor union that covered whatever tailor shops.
And he knew my mother.
He knew my father.
So he wanted to kind of do something for me.
So he got me out of that machine room where we're doing the sewing.
He got me into the part where they were doing with the cutting of the fabric.
Now, I was too young.
I wasn't a cutter.
I didn't know anything about cutting fabric.
So what I was doing, they had a huge table.
And they had the fabrics to lay out.
So at one time, it's like a big metal bar.
We put the fabric at one side the metal bar, pull the fabric to the other side of the table, roll it over and, like, lay it out in layers.
But there was a certain system of laying out of the fabric.
And then the cutters would come and put the patterns into the cutting.
So this was the nicer work.
It was a little bit easier.
It was a small group.
And I was the youngest, so the people had a little compassion.
They treated me like their child.
So that was the easier work.
Before that when my mother-- in 1942, my mother was taken away.
Again, she had those papers, those working papers because by that time, not everybody-- at the beginning, not everybody had jobs.
Only the lucky ones would get jobs.
And she felt so secure because she had them.
She was a meister.
A meister was, you know, a supervisor, a big fancy title that, you know, she's working.
My brother Laibele had tuberculosis.
And they surrounded the ghetto, and they came to take the sick, the old, and the children.
And we believed all the stories they told us.
We-- this sick are going to hospitals.
The old, they're going to places where they could work easier.
The children even get easy jobs.
But my mother didn't want them to take the-- her sick child.
She didn't trust them that much.
So she hid the sick child.
And when she went outside feeling very secure because she has working papers, she turned pale when she saw that they were looking at the house-- were going into the apartment.
And that's when she was taken away.
And I never saw her again.
This was in--
September.
Besides--
No.
We were literally dying from hunger.
Besides the overcrowding and the disease, the starvation-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was very little food.
We would have a loaf, a bread that was like a lump of clay.
It was made out of the other grains of the, the, you know, the shells, the grains that you would make flour or whatever.
So it came out like a lump of clay, the bread.
And this we had to divide for a whole week.
This-- we each had to stay in line to get the rations.
And if you couldn't divide it, you died even quicker because it takes a lot of strength to look at something in front of you when you're dying and say, I'm going to divide it.
I'll gonna to eat it tomorrow.
Not everybody could do it.
And we would get rations.
Most of the times the vegetables-- there were vegetables delivered to the ghetto.
They had a delaying day until it became rotten, and then they would give it out.
We would-- everything was rationed, was rationed to that point that people were literally dying from hunger.
I think it was the ghetto government.

I assumed it was the ghetto government because I think what the Nazis shipped into the ghetto-- and why they let that lay there and to become rotten-- if that was an order, if that was-- I don't know why they would let it do it that way, you know.
But as far as we knew, it was the ghetto government that would say this is the ration for this.
Sometimes for children, if there came in some skim milk, not regularly, but they would give it-- some extra ration to children.
If something came in, you know, they treated the children, at one time or another, a little, a little bit better.
[SIGHS] Well, my mother and my aunt when they were there, when we first moved-- when they moved in with us, because we were at-- in our place and they moved in with us.

My mother and my aunt used to do the cooking, take the rations that came in and divide it and tried to improvise and make it sound like this is a fancy cake you're eating, which was something from the coffee grinds.
They made a cake, but they called it a babka.
And yeah, they gave it real, real nice names, make it appetizing.
Or the skins of potatoes-- and they would-- well, now we know that it's healthy.
At that time, [LAUGHS] we didn't think about healthy.
It just was food.
And they would make cakes out of it and delicacies.
You know, the, the, the women were very ingenious and improvising with whatever, with whatever they could.
So they did the cooking.
Later, when everybody had to go to factories, there was nothing to cook.
The main meal was the bowl of soup in the factory and whatever.
We would get some bread or sometimes some margarine.
Or whatever the rations, we kind of divided them.
There really wasn't, wasn't much to cook.
No, she didn't have much to sell because whatever was of value, Mrs.
Gruber took it.
And there was nothin' to sell.

My brother said one time-- when my mother was taken away and I became their legal guardian, my brother sold their bread-- that was the valuable possession-- at one point to get vitamins for me-- [NON-ENGLISH], that was the name of the vitamins-- because my legs-- the, the loss of-- from malnutrition, the loss of calcium, I couldn't stand up.
I looked swollen.
I looked like I was fat.
But I couldn't stand up.
So they sold the ration of bread to get [NON-ENGLISH].
And at one point, they sold, again, the bread to get a tangerine on the black market.
And that food and the tangerine and the other were on the black market because the, the ghetto government had [INAUDIBLE] to the police and the ones in government.
And they would get the better rations.
So they could exchange it or trade it.
Usually there was no money trading-- you know, trading for something else.
So there was a black market going.
And they sold it for a tangerine, hoping that it's going to save my life again.
That took a lot of courage to do that.
And-- Which brothers were these?
Motele, Moishele, and Laibele.
If I was 16, Motele was 14.
Laibele was 13, and Moishele was 11, something within that range.
And one time, in fact, we were sitting there.
This morning, when I got dressed, my brother, well, he was visiting.
And he said, well, you don't look like you just came out of Auschwitz, into Auschwitz.
And I said, I came to Auschwitz wearing a suit because I put on a suit.
And I had an Uncle [NON-ENGLISH] that I [INAUDIBLE], my mother's brother.
He was my favorite uncle.
